game_name: Might and Magic: The Secret of the Inner Sanctum

danger_signals.visual_form: First-person perspective shift to combat mode; text-based enemy presentation
danger_signals.meaning: Transition from exploration to threat state; party is under immediate danger

danger_signals.visual_form: Forced shift from maze navigation to combat interface
danger_signals.meaning: Unpredictable danger zones within world terrain; spatial hazard

danger_signals.visual_form: Environmental landmark revealing sci-fi elements within fantasy setting
danger_signals.meaning: Presence of Sheltem threat; indicates narrative danger and world corruption

danger_signals.visual_form: Damage occurrence when entering specific city areas (Portsmith); character status change
danger_signals.meaning: Localized environmental hazard coded by character properties; world-state conditional danger

safety_signals.visual_form: Explorable indoor/walled environments in first-person perspective
safety_signals.meaning: Refuge zones; areas where party can rest and prepare

safety_signals.visual_form: Dialogue text options (run away, surrender, bribe, attack)
safety_signals.meaning: Negotiation possible; not all encounters are combat-mandatory

safety_signals.visual_form: Healing and defensive spells cast during or between encounters
safety_signals.meaning: Active party resilience; mitigation of danger state

faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Revealed as masquerade; Sheltem's true form as villain
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Royal court locations; throne room presence in castle exploration

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Mystery character identity; hunter of Sheltem
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Information discovered during quest progression; lore revelation

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Escaped prisoner; shapeshifter masquerading as authority
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: False King appearance; eventual unmasked form; escaped to Cron world
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Crashed spaceship crash site; Alien contact location; Inner Sanctum objective

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Maze-grid first-person navigation; walls represent terrain features (mountains, caves, trees, obstructions)
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Turn-based movement; player chooses forward/backward or 90-degree turns; no time passage during player decision

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Shift from exploration perspective to combat interface; text-based enemy display on most platforms
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Turn-based combat engaged; speed statistic determines action order; random speed bonuses applied per round

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Science fiction elements revealed (crashed spaceship, aliens, VARN true meaning); hidden threat beneath medieval fantasy facade
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Party discovers Sheltem masquerading as King; lore revelations; quest chain activated toward Inner Sanctum goal

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Gate transition to Another World; progression to Cron setting (Might and Magic II context)
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Party unaware Sheltem has also escaped; narrative continuation; world reset for sequel

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Localized area damage triggers; character status effects applied upon movement
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Males take injury in specific city zones; gender-fluid character modification available via world actions

environmental_storytelling.example: Maze-grid walls manifest as mountain ridges, cave stone, tree rows—visual language maps world geography through barrier design
Crashed spaceship site reveals sci-fi elements and Sheltem plot; aliens communicate threat and prisoner-at-large status
Castle and town placement communicates political centers; royal court presence establishes authority zones and deception sites
Portsmith gender-specific injury zones tell story of curse or magical protection; world-state embedded in geography
VARN acronym reveals vehicular/spacecraft nature; technology beneath medieval facade signals hidden world truth

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Grid-based maze navigation; 90-degree turning; forward/backward movement only; no time passage during decisions
Action selection from menu (attack, cast spell, run); turn order determined by speed statistic; subject to random speed bonuses
Offer negotiation options (surrender, bribe, attack) before forced combat; speed determines turn order; combat persistence until resolution
Appears as legitimate authority; maintains deception through official position and court presence
True form revealed; shifts from NPC authority to combat threat; defeated as final quest objective
Communicate Sheltem's escape and threat status; provide narrative exposition; mark world-state corruption

notes: Might and Magic NES (1992) uses minimal visual distinction for faction/world-state communication due to platform constraints. Most danger and faction information derives from narrative exposition and gameplay mode shifts rather than visual styling. The game presents world-state primarily through: (1) exploration vs. combat mode transition, (2) narrative revelations (crashed ship, Sheltem masquerade), (3) localized environmental hazards (Portsmith gender zones), and (4) architectural location hierarchy. Faction identity is communicated through plot progression rather than visual iconography. The game's hybrid medieval-fantasy/sci-fi setting itself serves as world-state tell—gradual exposure of VARN technology signals hidden truth of world nature.
